element_introduction_order.mastery_context: choosing between 3 American leaders (Washington, Ward, Lee) or 3 British leaders (Gage, Clinton, Howe) sets campaign difficulty and strategic approach
Continental Congress or British Parliament approves military spending distribution between navy, districts, debts, salaries—directly impacts campaign viability
purchasing materials, bribing generals, parades for support, training troops in controlled districts
Patriot/Loyalist militia and German mercenaries operate independently, can be recruited or absorbed by commanders
placement by entry district, terrain effects, unit positioning, combined arms with adjacent allied troops
Infantry (basic), Guerrillas (terrain traversal, surprise), Cavalry (shock charges), Engineers (artillery, bridge manipulation), Naval barrages (coastal districts)
civilian and political entity relationships determine budget ceiling; poor approval can result in removal as C-in-C
independent naval strategy management, coastal troop transport, naval barrages in combat

typical_relief_triggers: completing a turn without political crisis
typical_tension_triggers: quarterly budget meetings with uncertain approval
approaching enemy armies in adjacent districts
tactical battle engagement with multiple unit types
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Long-form strategic tension punctuated by quarterly budget cycles and tactical battle crises. Turn-based monthly intervals allow sustained planning pressure interspersed with discrete battle intensity spikes. Campaign-scale play spans 250+ hours with difficulty escalating as enemies consolidate positions.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Adjacent allied districts can provide reinforcements to battle locations; optional recruitment of autonomous militia/mercenary regiments in uncontrolled or border districts

difficulty_curve.early_game: American side: defensive positioning, gradual strength accumulation against British pressure. British side: fortify existing positions, rapidly consolidate early advantage.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Political approval fluctuations intensify; enemy commander consolidation increases district-to-district military pressure; budget constraints force strategic trade-offs
difficulty_curve.late_game: Attrition warfare dominates; remaining armies concentrated in key districts; political factors become critical to maintaining command authority; possibility of campaign defeat despite military progress
notable_spikes: First major tactical engagement introduces multiple unit types and terrain interactions
Enemy consolidation phase when autonomous militia begin coordinating attacks across districts
Political crisis threshold when approval falls below critical spending levels
Late-game numerical disadvantage when players face fully mobilized opponent armies

notable_level_patterns.description: Geographically contiguous district conquest with resource management feedback loop. Each controlled district generates resources but requires maintenance. Enemies control adjacent districts, creating natural conflict pressure.

Discrete battles occur when armies occupy the same district. Terrain hexes (with seasonal variation) create tactical positioning challenges. Unit type matchups and combined-arms doctrine determine outcomes.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Individual battle scenarios across the war campaign

notable_level_patterns.description: Quarterly congressional meetings force resource distribution decisions across navy, districts, and administration. Constraint-based planning creates emergent strategic problems.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Recurring throughout campaign, every 6 turns (3 months)
